data:
[1;38;5;208mSHELLTUTOR[0m  —  a vimtutor prerequisite course

Welcome. A short hands-on course in [1;38;5;244mfive stages[0m that
prepare you for [1;38;5;220mvimtutor[0m. About 45 to 60 minutes total.

Each stage is a few short lessons followed by a small task. Pass the
task to advance. Retry as much as you like — no time pressure, no
skip.

Your work happens in [38;5;109m/var/folders/jx/f_4n055973x5cwnyh_mhlw9h0000gn/T/shelltutor-sim-home-d43txb1k[0m; the tutor never writes
outside that folder.

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m for a quick orientation.[0m

[1;38;5;208mOrientation[0m

In a moment you'll be at the practice prompt below. It looks like:

    [2mshelltutor>[0m

Type a command, press Enter — the shell runs it. The terminal is
keyboard-only; you can't click mid-line to move the cursor. Resize
your terminal larger if text wraps (80×24 or bigger).

[1;38;5;244mFour words steer the tutor:[0m
  [1;38;5;220mnext[0m  advance        [1;38;5;220mprev[0m  go back
  [1;38;5;220mshow[0m  redisplay      [1;38;5;220mquit[0m  leave (progress saved)

A fifth word, [1;38;5;220mcheck[0m, appears at gate tasks when relevant.

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m to begin Stage 1.[0m

[1;38;5;208mSTAGE 1 — Where am I?[0m

Three short lessons (about 10 minutes) followed by a small task.
You'll meet the prompt, run a few simple commands, and learn the
keyboard habits that make the shell easy to live with.

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m to start lesson 1.1.[0m

[1;38;5;208m1.1 — running commands[0m

At the prompt below, try this:

  [1;38;5;220mecho hello[0m        [2m# type those words; press Enter[0m

You'll see [2mhello[0m printed. Then more:
  [1;38;5;220mecho I am at the shell[0m     [2m# spaces separate words[0m
  [1;38;5;220mecho "I am at the shell"[0m   [2m# quotes group them[0m

[1;38;5;244mFour keys to remember[0m:
  [38;5;208mTab[0m        bash finishes a word. Try [1;38;5;220mecho p[0m+[38;5;208mTab[0m → [1;38;5;220mecho poem.txt[0m.
             If several match, bash beeps; [38;5;208mTab[0m again lists them.
             (Bash doesn't cycle on repeated [38;5;208mTab[0m — zsh and fish do.)
  [38;5;208mCtrl+L[0m     clear screen — then [1;38;5;220mshow[0m brings this back.
  [38;5;208m↑[0m          recall the last command.
  [38;5;208mCtrl+C[0m     escape hatch if anything feels stuck.

[1;38;5;208m▶ Try a few, then type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m.[0m

[1;38;5;208m1.2 — where am I, who am I, when is it[0m

Three commands that ask the system about itself.

  [1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m     [2m# the user name you're logged in as[0m
  [1;38;5;220mpwd[0m        [2m# print working directory (the folder you're in)[0m
  [1;38;5;220mdate[0m       [2m# the current date and time[0m

In this practice prompt, [1;38;5;220mpwd[0m reports the tutor sandbox
folder. That's because the tutor [1;38;5;220mcd[0m'd here before handing
you the prompt — see Stage 2.

[1;38;5;244mCLEAR THE SCREEN[0m.  [38;5;208mCtrl+L[0m, or the command:
  [1;38;5;220mclear[0m

[1;38;5;208m▶ Run all three, then type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m.[0m

[1;38;5;208m1.3 — what just happened[0m

When you type [1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m and press Enter:

  1. The terminal hands your line to [1;38;5;244mthe shell[0m.
  2. The shell looks for a program named [1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m.
  3. It runs it.
  4. The program prints to [1;38;5;244mstandard output[0m — your screen.
  5. The shell shows the prompt again, ready for the next command.

The shell is not "Unix." It is a program — a [1;38;5;244mcommand interpreter[0m.
The terminal is a window that lets you talk to the shell. The program
[1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m is a third thing — it runs, prints, and exits.

You will keep these three apart for the rest of this tutor:
  [1;38;5;244mterminal[0m  →  [1;38;5;244mshell[0m  →  [1;38;5;244mprogram[0m

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m to take Stage 1's gate.[0m

[1;38;5;208mSTAGE 1 — GATE[0m

[1;38;5;244mRecall[0m (three questions, all three must be correct).

Then a [1;38;5;244mpractical task[0m: run a few commands and tell me what
they printed. The script verifies your answers.

[2mUnlimited retries. No skip.[0m
[1;38;5;39mQ1: Which character usually ends a shell prompt? ($, ;, :, or #)[0m

[1;38;5;39mQ3: After [1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m runs, where does its output go?[0m
  [1;38;5;220myour answer:[0m

[1;38;5;208mSTAGE 1 — GATE TASK[0m

Inside the practice prompt, run each of these once. Read each output.
Then type [1;38;5;220mcheck[0m.

  [1;38;5;220mwhoami[0m
  [1;38;5;220mpwd[0m
  [1;38;5;220mdate[0m

After all three have run, I'll ask you what each one printed.

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mcheck[1;38;5;208m only after running all three.[0m

[1;38;5;244mThree short follow-ups based on what you saw.[0m

[1;38;5;39mWhat did [1;38;5;220mwhoami[1;38;5;39m print? (just the username)[0m
  [1;38;5;220myour answer:[0m

[1;38;5;39mWhat did [1;38;5;220mpwd[1;38;5;39m print? (paste the full path)[0m
  [1;38;5;220myour answer:[0m

[1;38;5;39mWhat day of the week did [1;38;5;220mdate[1;38;5;39m show? (Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun)[0m
  [1;38;5;220myour answer:[0m

[1;38;5;208mSTAGE 2 — Paths and the filesystem[0m

Three lessons, then a gate. The shell is always [1;38;5;244msomewhere[0m in
the filesystem; this stage teaches you to move around and to predict
where files land.

You'll use:
  [1;38;5;220mls[0m    list what's in the current folder
  [1;38;5;220mcd[0m    change to another folder
  [1;38;5;220mpwd[0m   print where you are now

And the path symbols:
  [1;38;5;244m.[0m     this folder
  [1;38;5;244m..[0m    the parent folder
  [1;38;5;244m~[0m     your home folder

[1;38;5;208m▶ Type [1;38;5;220mnext[1;38;5;208m to begin lesson 2.1.[0m
Bye. Run 'shelltutor' again to pick up where you left off.
